channel.title: Agent Nouveau — Facebook
channel.description: Agent Nouveau journal — Facebook editorial feed

item.title: ⟡ The Safari That Stops Counting
item.description:
⟡ The Safari That Stops Counting
The "Big Five" was never a list of the best animals to watch. Big-game hunters grouped the lion, leopard, rhino, elephant and buffalo together more than a century ago for one reason... they were the five hardest and most dangerous to shoot on foot. The phrase that now sells safaris began life as a score sheet.
We are watching the appetite for the checklist quietly fade. Safari itineraries through American luxury advisors rose around twenty-two per cent year on year, per SmartFlyer's 2026 report. The number is rising. The trips behind it look less and less like the old circuit.
Our new journal piece on the quiet safari.
Come find us at No.82.
🕶️ ✉️ hello@agentnouveau.com

item.title: ⟡ One lion, one vehicle
item.description:
⟡ One lion, one vehicle
The quiet safari is not a sacrifice. It is usually the better seat. Botswana built a whole tourism model on the idea, holding lodge numbers, bed counts and vehicle density deliberately low across the Okavango Delta so the wildlife is never ringed by engines.
On many private concessions a sighting is held to two or three vehicles. Sometimes to one. A leopard watched by a single quiet car for an unhurried hour is a different memory from the same leopard glimpsed between strangers' shoulders. You begin to see behaviour rather than just the animal.
Come find us at No.82.
🕶️ ✉️ hello@agentnouveau.com

item.title: ⟡ Collect less, notice more
item.description:
⟡ Collect less, notice more
The old safari was a pursuit. Three or four parks in a week, long transfers, a guide under pressure to find all five animals before you flew home, and a dozen vehicles drawn up around one tired lion.
The new way slows down. Fewer places, longer stays, conservancies over crowded parks. The Africa Travel and Tourism Association calls it a move away from nature on demand toward deliberate, more embedded experiences. A morning in the bush should leave you fuller than it found you... not breathless from chasing a list someone else wrote.
Come find us at No.82.
🕶️ ✉️ hello@agentnouveau.com

item.title: ⟡ The Hours Before the Doors Open
item.description:
⟡ The Hours Before the Doors Open
A supper club lasts an evening. The room that holds it takes most of a day to make.
Long before the first guest finds the door at No.82, the work has been going on for hours. The everyday furniture moves aside, the long table comes to the centre, and for a while the room is bare again, waiting. It is our favourite hour, before the polish and the setting, when the whole evening exists only as an intention.
Our new journal piece on the quiet work before a supper club night.
Come find us at No.82.
🕶️ ✉️ hello@agentnouveau.com

item.title: ⟡ Setting the table
item.description:
⟡ Setting the table
The table is where the hours go. The linen is laid and smoothed. The glasses are set and turned to the light, then turned again. The candles are placed but not yet lit, waiting for the last moment before the doors open.
We set each place the same way, with the same attention, because the table is the first thing a guest meets and the thing they sit with all night. None of it is fussy. A table that says sit down, stay a while, you are looked after here.
Come find us at No.82.
🕶️ ✉️ hello@agentnouveau.com

item.title: ⟡ An evening of South African food, wine and stories
item.description:
⟡ An evening of South African food, wine and stories
On 25 June, from half past six, No.82 is given over to South Africa. An evening of South African food, wine and stories, shared in great company, hosted alongside South African travel specialist Marguerite Mornet.
For now there is only the room being readied for it... the table, the candles waiting to be lit, and a small gift at each place. We will keep the rest for the night itself. Some things are better found at the table than read about in advance.
Come find us at No.82.
🕶️ ✉️ hello@agentnouveau.com

item.title: ⟡ The Sachet in the Carry-On
item.description:
⟡ The Sachet in the Carry-On
A long-haul cabin holds roughly the same humidity as a desert. Eight hours of recycled air pulls moisture out of you steadily, which is why you land with dry skin and a flat head. Most of what we call jet lag begins, quietly, as dehydration.
It is the least glamorous thing that changes how a journey feels, and one of the most reliable. Lately, when we put a gift bag together at No.82, there is almost always a strip of Humantra sachets in it. A few grams of powder, six electrolytes, and a much better chance of arriving still feeling like yourself.
Our new journal piece on hydration as part of travelling well.
Come find us at No.82.
🕶️ ✉️ hello@agentnouveau.com

item.title: ⟡ Read the label out loud
item.description:
⟡ Read the label out loud
The sachet is Humantra, and what is in those few grams is the point. Six electrolytes... sodium, potassium, magnesium, calcium, phosphorus and chloride. The minerals your body runs on and loses through a hot day or a long flight, plus added Vitamin C, B12, zinc and chromium.
What is not in it matters too. No sugar, no artificial sweeteners or colours, no caffeine. The sweetness is stevia leaf, the flavours are real, and it is certified by Informed Sport. You can read the label out loud and understand all of it, which is rarer than it should be.
Come find us at No.82.
🕶️ ✉️ hello@agentnouveau.com

item.title: ⟡ One of the details we would not skip
item.description:
⟡ One of the details we would not skip
When a client books with us, the gift bag is tailored to the trip. A long-haul itinerary gets the things that long-haul takes out of you, and hydration sits at the top of that list.
Humantra earned its place there the way everything on the No.82 shelves does. We tried it, we read the back of the pack, and we kept reaching for it. A sachet weighs almost nothing and survives a flight folded into a passport wallet... one sachet, one bottle of water, and the most common drain on a travelling body is handled before you have finished boarding.
Come find us at No.82.
🕶️ ✉️ hello@agentnouveau.com

item.title: ⟡ Oro. Giudecca. Venice.
item.description:
⟡ Oro. Giudecca. Venice.
There is one restaurant in Venice you can only reach by water. Oro sits inside the Belmond Hotel Cipriani, at the tip of Giudecca, the quiet island across the lagoon from St Mark's. No bridge runs to it. You come by boat, and the hotel sends one for you.
The crossing takes a few minutes, and those minutes are half the meal. The city loosens its hold, the skyline rearranges itself into the right distance, and you arrive somewhere that has chosen calm on your behalf.
Our new journal piece on the table you reach by boat.
Come find us at No.82.
🕶️ ✉️ hello@agentnouveau.com

item.title: ⟡ A larder a few steps wide
item.description:
⟡ A larder a few steps wide
What lifts Oro out of the ordinary run of hotel dining rooms is where the food comes from. Much of it grows on the island. The Cipriani keeps its own organic gardens on Giudecca, sending salad leaves, herbs and edible flowers up to the kitchens through the day, picked rather than ordered.
The rest comes from the lagoon at the door, the moeche and the seasonal fish the city has eaten for centuries. The cooking does not have to travel far to be honest. It mostly just has to look down. 🖤
A table here is worth planning ahead for.
🕶️ ✉️ hello@agentnouveau.com

item.title: ⟡ A room called gold
item.description:
⟡ A room called gold
Oro means gold, and the room earns it. The dining room sits beneath a domed ceiling worked in gold leaf, lit by a handmade Murano-glass chandelier, the whole space turned by the designer Adam Tihany into something that glows rather than glitters.
It would be easy for a room like this to overplay its hand. It does not. The gold is the ceiling's business. At eye level the setting is calm, the linen plain, the windows giving onto the water and the lights of the city beyond.
Read the full story of Oro on our journal.
Come find us at No.82.
🕶️ ✉️ hello@agentnouveau.com

item.title: ⟡ The Hour Before the Heat
item.description:
⟡ The Hour Before the Heat
A safari wakes you at half past five, in the dark, with coffee you drink before you can see the cup. By half past six you are on the vehicle, wrapped in a blanket against a cold most people do not associate with Africa, watching the bush come back to itself as the light arrives.
It is the earliest most of our clients have risen in years, and almost without exception it becomes the part they miss most. Not the leopard. The quiet. The cold air. The hour before the heat, when nothing is asked of you and the whole day is still ahead.
Our new journal piece on the bush as a reset, and why the dawn is the point.
Come find us at No.82.
🕶️ ✉️ hello@agentnouveau.com

item.title: ⟡ A clock you do not keep yourself
item.description:
⟡ A clock you do not keep yourself
Most of a game drive is not pursuit. It is stillness. The vehicle stops, the engine goes off, and you sit. A guide reads the ground, points out a bird you would never have noticed, and lets the silence sit. There is no signal for your phone, and after the first morning you stop reaching for it.
That is the first kindness of the bush. The clock is being kept for you... which means, for the first time in a long while, you are not keeping it yourself. The falling-in is the whole therapy.
Come find us at No.82.
🕶️ ✉️ hello@agentnouveau.com

item.title: ⟡ The middle of the day is empty by design
item.description:
⟡ The middle of the day is empty by design
Between the two drives there is a stretch of hours with nothing in them. At home that would feel like waste. In the bush it feels like the point. The heat climbs, the animals lie up in the shade, and the camp goes soft and slow.
This enforced pause is the thing modern travel has quietly engineered out. We fill the gaps. We optimise the day. We come home from a holiday needing a holiday. The bush does not offer that option, and a few days of it resets something a city break leaves untouched.
Come find us at No.82.
🕶️ ✉️ hello@agentnouveau.com

item.title: ⟡ The Wine Became the Reason
item.description:
⟡ The Wine Became the Reason
Global wine tourism is now a sector worth around fifty-one billion dollars a year. The interesting part is not the number. It is what the number describes.
For a long time, wine was a stop on a holiday. A morning out from the city, a tasting on a schedule, a clock running over the whole thing. That has quietly changed. Wine tourism now accounts for roughly a quarter of global winery revenue, which means the estates that pay attention are building the visit itself, not just selling the bottle. The wine, the valley and the table have become the reason for the journey.
Our new Travel Intelligence piece on wine travel growing up.
Come find us at No.82.
🕶️ ✉️ hello@agentnouveau.com

item.title: ⟡ The day trip and the stay
item.description:
⟡ The day trip and the stay
There are two ways to travel for wine, and the difference is the whole thing.
The day trip extracts. A morning out from the city, a tasting booked to the minute, a designated driver who cannot taste a thing, and a clock running over the table. The stay restores. It gives the valley enough hours to register, lets a lunch run its natural length, and asks nothing of the afternoon afterwards.
This year Virtuoso, who plan for travellers much like ours, reported that dining has moved from a perk to a pillar of the trip. People are no longer fitting wine into a holiday. They are building one around it... and we think they are right to.
Come find us at No.82.
🕶️ ✉️ hello@agentnouveau.com

item.title: ⟡ Where this lands
item.description:
⟡ Where this lands
In South Africa, wine tourism contributed 9.3 billion rand to the economy and made up around seventeen per cent of the wine industry's turnover. Six Cape vineyards now sit among the world's fifty best.
The valleys an hour east of Cape Town, around Stellenbosch and Franschhoek, are built for exactly the kind of travel the figures keep pointing towards. They run on long lunches and late light. They reward staying in the valley over driving out for the day. And they fall outside the European summer, which is its own quiet argument for going. That is the thread we are following through June.
Come find us at No.82.
🕶️ ✉️ hello@agentnouveau.com

item.title: ⟡ The Grape That Belongs Nowhere Else
item.description:
⟡ The Grape That Belongs Nowhere Else
The first wine at the Cape was pressed on the second of February, 1659. We know the date because the man responsible wrote it down in his diary, in the plain relief of someone who had been waiting. South Africa is one of the few wine countries that can name the morning it began.
It is usually filed under New World wine and sold on price, which misses almost everything. By 1685 the sweet wine of Constantia was travelling to the courts of Europe... the first New World wine to win real international acclaim, a hundred years before most of the famous names had planted a vine.
Our new journal piece on a wine country with its own grapes and its own mind.
Come find us at No.82.
🕶️ ✉️ hello@agentnouveau.com

item.title: ⟡ The white that made it home
item.description:
⟡ The white that made it home
For most of its life at the Cape it answered to another name. Steen, they called it, and they planted more of it than anything else without quite knowing what it was. The vines were not confidently identified as French Chenin Blanc until 1965, by which time the Cape had been growing it for three hundred years and had made it, in every sense that matters, its own.
More than half of all the Chenin Blanc in the world now grows in South Africa, more than in the Loire valley where it began. The Cape did not borrow this grape so much as adopt it and raise it as its own.
Come find us at No.82.
🕶️ ✉️ hello@agentnouveau.com

item.title: ⟡ The one that exists nowhere else
item.description:
⟡ The one that exists nowhere else
In 1925 a professor at Stellenbosch named Abraham Perold crossed Pinot Noir with Cinsaut and planted four seeds in his garden. He very nearly lost them. The seedlings were rescued from a clearance by a young lecturer who happened to be passing, and from those vines came Pinotage, a grape that grows seriously almost nowhere on earth but here.
It has not always been easy to love, and that is part of the point. No one argues about a wine with no character. The Cape has a red that belongs to it completely, the way few countries can say of any grape, and the best of it now is very good indeed.
Come find us at No.82.
🕶️ ✉️ hello@agentnouveau.com

item.title: ⟡ The Trip That Earns Its Distance
item.description:
⟡ The Trip That Earns Its Distance
Long-haul destinations grew their share of UK holidays from seven per cent to ten in a single year. Australia, New Zealand, the Pacific Islands... places measured in days of flying, not hours. That figure comes from ABTA's Travel Trends 2026, and it is not a blip.
The far edge of the map is having a quiet revival. Our new Travel Intelligence piece looks at why the considered traveller is choosing one big trip over several small ones, and where that instinct leads.
Come find us at No.82.
🕶️ ✉️ hello@agentnouveau.com

item.title: ⟡ Fewer trips, more room in each
item.description:
⟡ Fewer trips, more room in each
The 2026 Virtuoso Luxe Report describes travellers slowing down. Lingering longer once they arrive, choosing smaller camps and longer stays over a packed itinerary. Two-thirds of their advisors expect demand to rise next year, and more than half expect clients to spend more per trip.
People are not necessarily travelling more often. They are travelling further, and giving each trip more room. A twenty-four hour journey has never made sense for four nights. It makes a great deal of sense for two unhurried weeks.
Come find us at No.82.
🕶️ ✉️ hello@agentnouveau.com

item.title: ⟡ The distance is part of the point
item.description:
⟡ The distance is part of the point
South Africa is a long way to go, and that is precisely why it rewards the traveller who stays. A fortnight there, the city and the winelands and the long slow afternoons, returns more than a string of short hops ever could.
The long trip is not an indulgence. It is the better use of the same time and the same money. Further, and then slower once you land... and you come home restored, rather than barely having had time to feel the place at all.
Come find us at No.82.
🕶️ ✉️ hello@agentnouveau.com

item.title: ⟡ The Road That Is the Holiday
item.description:
⟡ The Road That Is the Holiday
Knysna and Plettenberg Bay sit barely thirty kilometres apart. Half an hour of driving, the two best-known towns on South Africa's Garden Route close enough to share a morning.
Which is the thing most people get wrong about the route. They treat it as a list of stops to collect before dark. We would make the case for the opposite. The road is the holiday. The forest, the lagoons, the mountains on one side and the ocean on the other... the drive is not how you reach the experience. It is the experience.
Our new journal piece on the Garden Route, and the case for travelling it slowly.
Come find us at No.82.
🕶️ ✉️ hello@agentnouveau.com

item.title: ⟡ The best part has no name
item.description:
⟡ The best part has no name
Wilderness to Knysna is about forty-five kilometres, the road running past a chain of lakes and lagoons close enough to the verge to touch. Then Knysna to Plettenberg Bay, thirty more, the forest thickening and thinning, the ocean appearing and slipping behind a rise and appearing again.
Driven in a hurry, it is forgettable. Driven slowly, with the windows down and nowhere to be by a fixed hour, it is the reason to come. The towns at either end are lovely. The road between them is the point.
Come find us at No.82.
🕶️ ✉️ hello@agentnouveau.com

item.title: ⟡ Give the road the days
item.description:
⟡ Give the road the days
The common mistake is the schedule. Three towns in a day, a photograph at each, a long drive after dark to make the next booking. The road punishes that by becoming invisible, just the dull bit between the parts that count.
Travelled the other way, with two or three nights in one place and the road itself treated as the day's plan, the whole thing changes. The mornings are slow. The drives are short. The light arrives in the late afternoon and asks nothing of you but that you stop and watch it. You come home slower than you left... and that is the version worth planning.
Come find us at No.82.
🕶️ ✉️ hello@agentnouveau.com

item.title: ⟡ The Valleys That Run on Afternoons
item.description:
⟡ The Valleys That Run on Afternoons
The first wine at the Cape was pressed in 1659. The vineyards that grew from it cover the valleys an hour east of Cape Town, and after three and a half centuries they have learned how to be unhurried.
We keep sending people to Stellenbosch and Franschhoek, and almost never for the scores. We send them for the afternoon. A table booked for one o'clock that is still going at five. The light coming down off the mountains in the last hours of the day. Travel measured in hours given back, not sights collected.
Our new journal piece on Franschhoek, Stellenbosch, and the case for the long lunch.
Come find us at No.82.
🕶️ ✉️ hello@agentnouveau.com

item.title: ⟡ Lunch as the destination
item.description:
⟡ Lunch as the destination
Franschhoek takes its name from the French corner, after the Huguenots who arrived in 1688 with vine cuttings, surnames that still appear on the labels, and a way of eating that took its time. The valley kept the habit.
A good long lunch starts late and refuses to hurry. A table on a shaded stoep, courses arriving when they are ready rather than when the kitchen wants the table back, a bottle from the estate you are sitting on, and a view that does most of the talking. By the time coffee comes the afternoon has gone, and that was the plan.
Come find us at No.82.
🕶️ ✉️ hello@agentnouveau.com

item.title: ⟡ Stay in the valley
item.description:
⟡ Stay in the valley
The common mistake is basing yourself in Cape Town and driving out for the day. An hour each way, a designated driver who cannot taste a thing, and a clock running over the whole table.
The Winelands reward the opposite. Stay in the valley. Give it days rather than hours. Let the afternoons run their natural length, with no drive home hanging over the last glass. Travelled this way, the region restores rather than depletes... and that is the version worth planning.
Come find us at No.82.
🕶️ ✉️ hello@agentnouveau.com
